hook: You already know how to follow directions like 'If it is raining, then I bring an umbrella.' That sentence feels completely logical. But here is where it gets tricky: does it also mean that if you bring an umbrella, it must be raining? These two statements look related, but are they saying the same thing? What happens when we flip a logical statement around, and does flipping it always preserve its truth?
concept: A conditional statement is an 'if-then' statement that links a condition to a conclusion. In formal notation, we write it as $p \Rightarrow q$, read 'if $p$, then $q$,' where $p$ is the hypothesis and $q$ is the conclusion. The converse is formed by switching the hypothesis and conclusion: $q \Rightarrow p$. A conditional and its converse are logically independent — one can be true while the other is false.
anatomy: Every conditional statement $p \Rightarrow q$ has two named parts: the hypothesis $p$ (the condition that comes right after the word 'if') and the conclusion $q$ (the result that follows 'then'). The converse $q \Rightarrow p$ is a new statement built by swapping those two roles — what was the conclusion becomes the new hypothesis, and what was the hypothesis becomes the new conclusion. A conditional can be true or false, and its converse is a completely separate statement whose truth value must be evaluated independently.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Conditional: If a polygon is a square, then it has four sides.}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying the full conditional statement. Notice the structure: 'If [something], then [something else].' This is the standard form $p \Rightarrow q$ that we will work with throughout the lesson.

worked_example.expression_latex: p: \text{a polygon is a square} \quad q: \text{it has four sides}
worked_example.description_latex: Now we label the hypothesis $p$ and the conclusion $q$ separately. Isolating these two parts is the critical skill, because forming the converse is simply a matter of swapping their positions.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Converse: If a polygon has four sides, then it is a square.}
worked_example.description_latex: We form the converse by placing $q$ in the 'if' position and $p$ in the 'then' position, giving us $q \Rightarrow p$. Notice how the sentence structure changes — the conclusion of the original is now the hypothesis of the converse.

worked_example.expression_latex: p \Rightarrow q: \text{ TRUE} \qquad q \Rightarrow p: \text{ FALSE}
worked_example.description_latex: Finally, we evaluate the truth value of each statement independently. The original is true because every square does have four sides. The converse is false because not every four-sided polygon is a square — a rectangle is a counterexample. This shows that a true conditional does not guarantee a true converse.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Conditional: If two angles are vertical angles, then they are congruent.}
partial_example.description_latex: We are given a conditional statement. Let's begin by reading it carefully and identifying which part is the hypothesis and which is the conclusion before we do anything else.

partial_example.expression_latex: p: \text{two angles are vertical angles} \quad q: \text{they are congruent}
partial_example.description_latex: Here the hypothesis $p$ and conclusion $q$ have already been labeled for you. Use these to build the converse by swapping their roles — write out the full 'If..., then...' sentence for the converse $q \Rightarrow p$.
Now determine whether the converse you wrote is true or false, and if it is false, provide a specific counterexample — a pair of congruent angles that are NOT vertical angles.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Identify the hypothesis and conclusion: } \textit{If } x = 5, \text{ then } x^2 = 25.
\text{Write the converse of: If a figure is a triangle, then it has exactly three angles.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Converse: If a figure has exactly three angles, then it is a triangle.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{State whether the conditional and its converse are true or false:} \\ \text{Conditional: If } x > 0, \text{ then } x^2 > 0.
practice_problems.answer_latex: p \Rightarrow q: \text{ TRUE} \quad q \Rightarrow p: \text{ FALSE (counterexample: } x = -3, x^2 = 9 > 0 \text{ but } x < 0\text{)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Given: If two lines are perpendicular, then they form a } 90^{\circ} \text{ angle.} \\ \text{Write the converse. Then decide: if both the conditional AND its converse are true, what special term applies to that biconditional relationship?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Converse: If two lines form a } 90^\circ \text{ angle, then they are perpendicular. Both are TRUE, so the biconditional } p \Leftrightarrow q \text{ holds.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A sign reads: ``If you are a student, you receive a discount.'' The cashier says: ``You received a discount, so you must be a student.'' Is the cashier's reasoning valid? Identify the logical error using conditional statement vocabulary.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{The cashier is asserting the converse } q \Rightarrow p \text{. This is invalid because the converse of a true conditional is not necessarily true — other groups may also receive the discount.}

common_mistakes: Students often assume the converse is automatically true whenever the conditional is true. The fix: always test the converse with a specific counterexample before declaring it true — they are logically independent statements.

When writing the converse, students sometimes negate the hypothesis or conclusion instead of simply swapping them, accidentally writing the inverse ($\lnot p \Rightarrow \lnot q$) instead of the converse ($q \Rightarrow p$). The fix: the converse requires NO negation — only exchange the positions of $p$ and $q$.

Students confuse the hypothesis and conclusion when the conditional is written in non-standard word order, such as 'A number is even if it is divisible by 2,' where 'divisible by 2' is actually $p$. The fix: rewrite the statement in 'If..., then...' form first, then label $p$ and $q$.
untested_variants: Writing the inverse ($\lnot p \Rightarrow \lnot q$) and contrapositive ($\lnot q \Rightarrow \lnot p$) of a conditional statement and comparing their truth values to the original.
Combining a true conditional with its true converse into a formal biconditional statement written with 'if and only if' ($p \Leftrightarrow q$) and interpreting what that means geometrically.
Identifying whether a given geometric theorem is stated as a conditional and determining whether its converse is also a theorem (e.g., the Isosceles Triangle Theorem and its converse).
Constructing a truth table for $p \Rightarrow q$ and $q \Rightarrow p$ to formally demonstrate that they have different truth values when $p$ is false and $q$ is true.
